title: Pax Autocratica

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Strong Steam store text describes a hybrid centered on building and governing a colony/state apparatus, expanding facilities, researching technologies, automating production and logistics, managing population conditions, and then personally leading first-person roguelite combat expeditions. That supports colony_sim as the best-fit primary identity, with military_fps and RTS-like command elements as secondary influences. Evidence is somewhat noisy because the Metacritic description appears mismatched to another game, but the Steam detailed and short descriptions are concrete and substantial enough on their own to support acceptance.

overall_note: The proposed rail has one solid candidate that captures the anchor's build-expand-defend sci-fi colony loop plus active combat, but it misses the anchor's distinctive first-person roguelite campaign structure and governance layer.
anchor_summary: Pax Autocratica is a hybrid colony-management/base-building game with population policy and logistics layers that periodically shifts into direct first-person roguelite combat expeditions in a sci-fi setting.
gap_notes: Candidate matches the base-building, resource logistics, tech progression, and active combat mix well.
Biggest gap is the anchor's identity as a colony sim plus first-person roguelite hybrid; this candidate is more action base-defense/survival than regime-management FPS.
The rail would benefit from more games that combine management/building with direct embodied combat, especially first-person or squad-leading hybrids.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Strong fit on sci-fi base building, resource chains, research, expansion, and real-time direct combat. It is closer to action base-defense/survival than to Pax Autocratica’s citizen-governance and first-person roguelite structure, so not quite the same gameplay identity.

It is closer to action base-defense/survival than to Pax Autocratica’s citizen-governance and first-person roguelite structure, so not quite the same gameplay identity.

title: Rotwood

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Rotwood as a 1-4 player hack-and-slash roguelike/dungeon crawler built around repeat runs, co-op combat, boss fights, weapon mastery, gear upgrading, and bringing spoils back to craft equipment and improve a safe haven. The text explicitly says 'every run offers different powers and abilities,' calls it a 'rogue-like dungeon crawler,' emphasizes combat mastery over randomness, and highlights increasingly difficult encounters and powerful corrupted bosses. Metacritic also labels it a '2D Beat-'Em-Up,' which supports the side-scrolling/beat-em-up secondary read, but the stronger identity is cooperative action roguelite due to run-based structure, repeatable progression, and meta/base advancement.

overall_note: The candidate set captures Rotwood’s co-op action-roguelite side well at the top, especially with games centered on repeat runs, melee combat, and boss progression. The weaker half drifts toward broader co-op action or classic dungeon crawling that misses Rotwood’s specific mix of run-based structure, combat mastery, and brawler-like feel.
anchor_summary: Rotwood is a 1-4 player co-op action roguelite/hack-and-slash built around repeat runs, weapon mastery, build variation, boss-centric encounters, gear progression, and skill-based combat in a 2D brawler-friendly format.
gap_notes: The strongest matches are co-op action roguelites with melee emphasis and boss/run progression.
There is only one strong candidate that clearly reflects Rotwood’s 2D brawler/beat-'em-up lineage.
Some candidates match co-op combat broadly but are looser on progression structure or combat identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: 1-4 player co-op action roguelite with hack-and-slash combat, weapon/build experimentation, room-to-room runs, and boss-driven progression.
Strong match on co-op run-based action, distinct combat styles, replayable builds, and difficult fights, though it is more top-down roguelike and less brawler-like than Rotwood.
Excellent comp for the 4-player side-scrolling hack-and-slash/brawler identity, with boss-heavy stage progression; less roguelite and build/run-focused than Rotwood.
Shares co-op-capable action RPG roguelite structure, persistent progression, and dungeon runs, but it is more story-driven and character-switching ARPG than combat-mastery brawler.
Understandable as a 4-player co-op combat crawler comp, but it is a broader class-based dungeon action game and misses Rotwood’s specific weapon-mastery, boss-centric roguelite identity.

payload.anchor_summary: Rotwood is a 1-4 player co-op action roguelite/hack-and-slash built around repeat runs, weapon mastery, build variation, boss-centric encounters, gear progression, and skill-based combat in a 2D brawler-friendly format.

title: NINJA GAIDEN 4 The Two Masters

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DLC descriptions consistently describe a combat-centric action game built around three new campaign chapters, unique levels, new weapons, bosses, fiend enemies, deadly trials, and an endurance-style challenge mode with successive combat encounters. The weapon copy emphasizes ranged attack, crowd control, rapid strikes, closing distance, and testing skilled 'master ninja' play, which strongly supports a fast, mastery-heavy character-action identity. The campaign/chapter structure and boss/trial language support linear level-based progression rather than open-world or RPG-first play. Horror imagery is present in the setting and story framing, but the gameplay evidence is much more strongly centered on stylish high-intensity combat than on horror as the primary play identity.

overall_note: The rail has the essential inclusion in the base game and two credible character-action neighbors. It is directionally correct, but beyond the base game the set leans more toward broader stylish-action comps than near-identical Ninja Gaiden-style fits.
anchor_summary: NINJA GAIDEN 4 The Two Masters is a direct DLC extension of a fast, mission/chapter-based character-action game: high-speed melee combat, weapon-specific movesets, boss encounters, trials, and an endurance challenge mode built around precision and mastery.
gap_notes: The base game is an essential inclusion and clearly the best match.
The remaining candidates are good character-action comparisons, but they are genre neighbors rather than especially tight DLC-to-gameplay matches.
The set is small and becomes noticeably looser after the base game.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game for this DLC, inheriting the same campaign structure, combat systems, weapons, enemies, and challenge-oriented progression.
Very strong character-action comp with mission-based progression, demanding enemy encounters, distinct weapon/playstyle kits, and replayable stylish combat mastery.

Another premier stylish action match with fast dodge-combo combat, set-piece battles, and score/challenge-driven replay, though its combat feel is a bit more spectacle-forward than Ninja Gaiden’s harsher precision.

title: Apopia: Sugar Coated Tale

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player hand-drawn narrative adventure with puzzles, rhythm segments, minigames, light action, and character-focused mystery. However, the copy does not establish a stable core gameplay loop, perspective, traversal model, or dominant mechanics strongly enough to support any allowed V2 archetype. It is not clearly a visual novel, hidden-object puzzle game, action-platformer, psychological horror title, or cinematic action-adventure based on the available text. The gameplay appears intentionally mixed and provider copy stays broad, so the evidence is insufficient for a confident archetype assignment.

overall_note: The rail mostly understands the anchor as a story-driven indie adventure with whimsical presentation, emotional mystery, and mechanic variety. Best fits are games that combine character-focused exploration with puzzles or music/minigame interactions. A few candidates lean too far toward RPG combat systems or near-visual-novel interactive story structures versus Apopia’s broader adventure-plus-minigame profile.
anchor_summary: Apopia: Sugar Coated Tale appears to be a single-player, hand-drawn narrative adventure built around exploration, puzzles, dialogue, minigames/rhythm beats, light action, and a cute exterior that gives way to darker mystery and identity themes.
gap_notes: The set skews heavily toward tonal similarity; not all candidates match Apopia's mixed adventure/puzzle/rhythm/light-action loop equally well.
There is no especially tight comp for the anchor's specific blend of dialogue, puzzles, rhythm elements, and enemy-outsmarting minigames.
Some candidates are stronger on narrative structure than on hands-on play variety, which makes them adjacent rather than truly close gameplay matches.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very good match on whimsical character-driven adventure, exploration, puzzle solving, and music-led interactions with emotional payoff; its platforming emphasis is a mild mismatch.
Strong fit for narrative-first exploration, rich NPC interactions, minigame variety, and slow-burn darker themes, though it is lighter on puzzle/rhythm-centric progression than Apopia.

Shares quirky writing, emotional mystery, and conflict resolved through distinctive encounter mechanics, but its core identity is much more RPG/bullet-dodge combat driven than Apopia's broader adventure-minigame structure.

Good tonal and narrative match with cute-creepy mystery and choice-driven story discovery, but its branching storybook structure is closer to interactive narrative than Apopia's puzzle/rhythm/light-action mix.

Understandable comp for childlike perspective, cute-macabre contrast, and simple exploration, but it is more of a linear interactive story with limited gameplay depth compared with Apopia's more varied adventure systems.

title: Scott Pilgrim EX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Classification is strongly supported by the stored store/editorial copy plus a direct Metacritic genre label of '2D Beat-'Em-Up.' The text repeatedly frames the game as a 'brand-new brawling adventure' centered on fighting through Toronto, stringing together stylish combos, using weapons and special attacks, battling enemies and bosses, and supporting up to four-player co-op. It also specifies a 2D beat-'em-up identity, seven fighters with distinct combat styles, and a city of interconnected levels with quests and hidden areas, which fits a campaign-driven beat_em_up with semi-open progression and light action-adventure structure rather than a pure linear stage arcade brawler. Progression is reinforced by badges, stat upgrades, coins, and customizable builds.

overall_note: The rail is solid and correctly focused on modern beat-'em-ups. Its strongest hit is River City Girls 2 because it mirrors not just the brawling but also the connected-world, quest, shop, and progression structure. The rest are valid genre neighbors, though TMNT and Streets of Rage 4 skew more arcade-linear than the anchor, and Castle Crashers is a looser but still understandable co-op brawler comp.
anchor_summary: Scott Pilgrim EX is a modern 2D beat-'em-up centered on combo-driven melee combat, multiple distinct fighters, light build/stat progression, quests, interconnected city exploration, and up-to-4-player drop-in co-op. Best matches should preserve the side-scrolling brawler core loop first, with extra credit for campaign progression, explorable structure, and party co-op.
gap_notes: The set lacks the obvious Scott Pilgrim benchmark: Scott Pilgrim vs. The World: The Game would be an expected must-include if available.
The current list leans heavily on arcade-stage beat-'em-ups; the anchor's interconnected-city exploration and quest structure are only strongly reflected by River City Girls 2.
A stronger rail would balance pure genre comps with more progression-forward, semi-open campaign brawlers.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: side-scrolling co-op beat-'em-up combat plus leveling, shops, items, quests, multiple fighters, and a connected city to explore rather than only linear stages.

Strong genre neighbor for side-scrolling brawling, character move sets, specials, and co-op, but it tracks even more toward classic stage-based arcade structure and away from the anchor's questing and connected-world adventure layer.

Understandable co-op brawler match with four-player chaos and light RPG-style progression, but its combat feel and arcade-adventure structure are broader and less specifically aligned with Scott Pilgrim EX's urban combo-brawler plus interconnected exploration setup.

title: Esoteric Ebb

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Esoteric Ebb as an isometric single-player CRPG with deep branching dialogue, abundant choices, open-ended quests, dice-driven TTRPG-style encounters, investigation of a political conspiracy, and exploration of a fantasy city. The strongest identity signals are authored branching narrative, quest-solving, detective-mystery framing, and classic CRPG structure rather than party tactics or action combat. Combat is explicitly de-emphasized ('violence as a last resort'), so western_narrative_rpg fits better than party_based_crpg or combat-forward RPG archetypes.

overall_note: This candidate set is very strong and captures the anchor's dialogue-first, investigation-led CRPG identity well. Disco Elysium is an obvious essential benchmark, with the Torment games as close philosophical/text-heavy CRPG neighbors. Citizen Sleeper and Pentiment are looser but still credible because they share choice-driven investigation and low-combat narrative progression.
anchor_summary: Esoteric Ebb is a single-player isometric narrative CRPG with strong Disco Elysium DNA: investigation-heavy questing, branching dialogue, internal stat/voice interplay, dice-driven checks, failure-forward roleplay, and combat treated as a secondary or last-resort solution.
gap_notes: The rail is strongest on dialogue-first narrative RPGs, but it is somewhat light on other modern isometric CRPGs with explicit dice-check/skill-check quest structure.
Only one candidate really matches the anchor's specific 'stats/voices in your head' and failure-forward detective loop exactly; the rest trend broader narrative RPG or classic text-heavy CRPG.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-perfect comp: isometric detective RPG, internalized skill voices, heavy dialogue checks, political/murder investigation, and failure-forward tabletop-style roleplay over combat.

Very close on isometric narrative CRPG structure, text-heavy encounters, stat checks, consequential choices, and strange-setting exploration, though it is more party-based and combat-present than the anchor.
A foundational dialogue-heavy CRPG with bizarre worldbuilding and conversation-first problem solving, but its classic party-based combat structure is more central than in Esoteric Ebb.

Strong overlap in dice-driven decisions, faction navigation, authored narrative progression, and low-combat roleplay, but it is structurally more of a narrative survival/life-management RPG than a classic isometric CRPG.
Shares mystery-solving, dialogue-led investigation, and consequential authored choices, but lacks the CRPG stat-check/build identity and tabletop-style systemic roleplaying that define the anchor.

title: iRacing Arcade

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store copy is strong and concrete: the game is repeatedly described as a 'third-person arcade racer' focused on licensed car racing, online races, global time trials, and weekly events. Core play is competing in races, chasing lap times, and progressing through a motorsport legacy structure by earning resources and investing in team growth, but the identity remains vehicular racing first. The 'easy to learn yet endlessly rewarding' physics, stylized recreations of licensed cars/tracks, and explicit arcade-racer phrasing support arcade_racer over realistic_racer, while the management layer appears secondary rather than transforming it into a sim/tycoon game.

overall_note: The rail understands the anchor as circuit-based motorsport racing rather than open-world driving, and its best candidate matches the anchor's arcade-plus-management identity well. The rest skew noticeably more sim/simcade, so the set is usable but leans away from the anchor's approachable arcade feel and secondary management loop.
anchor_summary: iRacing Arcade is an accessible track-focused motorsport racer: arcade-leaning handling, licensed cars/tracks, online races and time trials, plus a light team-building/career management layer between events.
gap_notes: Only one candidate strongly reflects the anchor's distinctive mix of arcade circuit racing and between-race team/resource progression.
The set over-indexes on realism-heavy motorsport sims/simcades compared with the anchor's explicitly arcade-forward handling and stylized presentation.
There is no must-keep benchmark here that mirrors the anchor's full identity especially closely.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best fit: accessible arcade-style motorsport racing with career seasons, upgrades, staff/team management, time trials, and on-track/off-track decision making very close to the anchor's loop.

Strong circuit-racing and multiplayer overlap, with accessible handling and career structure, but it is more spectacle-driven simcade racing and lacks the anchor's team-building/resource-management emphasis.

Licensed track racing, lap mastery, progression, and online competition fit broadly, but the core identity is much more simulation-focused and builder/tuning-oriented than the anchor's arcade-first motorsport loop.

There is shared overlap in circuit racing, time trials, progression, and large licensed car/track rosters, but Gran Turismo 7 is fundamentally a realistic driving sim with collection/tuning depth rather than an arcade-forward racer with light team management.

title: Legacy of Kain: Defiance Remastered

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently depict a third-person single-player action-adventure campaign centered on two protagonists with distinct combat abilities, battling enemies through ruined gothic spaces while solving environmental puzzles and progressing through an authored dark-fantasy story. The text strongly supports melee/supernatural combat, handcrafted linear-to-semi-open level spaces, puzzle gating, and long-form campaign structure, which best fits cinematic_action_adventure. A light action_platformer adjacency is present due to traversal/platform navigation, but platforming does not appear primary.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's story-driven third-person action-adventure identity fairly well, with one especially strong franchise match and two solid genre neighbors. The biggest gap is the lack of more direct comps centered on puzzle-forward 3D action-adventure structure from the same era/style.
anchor_summary: Legacy of Kain: Defiance Remastered is a single-player third-person cinematic action-adventure built around melee/supernatural combat, authored dark-fantasy progression, environmental puzzles, traversal/platforming, and linear-to-semi-linear exploration across handcrafted spaces.
gap_notes: The Soul Reaver remaster is the clearest must-keep comp because it shares franchise lineage, protagonist overlap, puzzle structure, and close moment-to-moment gameplay identity.
Darksiders III fits the third-person combat-plus-exploration template, but its more Soulslike challenge model and open-ended structure make it less exact than the best match.

Castlevania: Lords of Shadow is a credible dark-fantasy cinematic action-adventure neighbor, though its combat spectacle and pacing are somewhat more combo-driven and less tightly aligned with Defiance's specific identity.

candidate_reviews.candidate_title: Legacy of Kain: Soul Reaver 1 & 2 Remastered
candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest match by far: same franchise lineage, Raziel-led 3D action-adventure, realm/puzzle focus, supernatural melee powers, and similarly authored exploration.
Strong third-person dark-fantasy action-adventure comp with linear campaign structure, melee combat, traversal, and environmental puzzle sequences across large handcrafted spaces.
Shares third-person melee combat, dungeon-like exploration, traversal unlocks, and puzzle elements, but its more open progression and harsher Soulslike challenge model make it a looser fit.

Shares third-person melee combat, dungeon-like exploration, traversal unlocks, and puzzle elements, but its more open progression and harsher Soulslike challenge model make it a looser fit.

title: Go! Go! Mister Chickums

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 'classic single-screen platformer'/'2D platformer' with '100 lovingly designed screens,' explicit 'jump and run' play, local 2-player couch co-op, and arcade-inspired leaderboard chasing. The copy also states players 'finish off the numerous enemies with their very own challenges and tactics' and confront the villain Grabbo, which is enough to establish meaningful combat alongside dominant platform traversal. This supports action_platformer over other platformer families: it is clearly not a metroidvania, not a 3D collectathon, and the emphasis is broader arcade stage-clearing rather than precision-only challenge branding.

overall_note: The rail has one excellent gameplay match, one understandable but genre-shifted neighbor, and one clearly off-target pick driven more by franchise/history than by the actual game listed.
anchor_summary: Go! Go! Mister Chickums is a classic arcade-style single-screen 2D platformer built around compact stage clears, enemy handling, item/egg collection, timer pressure, leaderboard chasing, and local co-op.
gap_notes: The best-fit lane here is single-screen arcade platformers with enemy-clearing and couch co-op; only Bubble Bobble 4 Friends really lands there.
Toki Tori overlaps on compact 2D egg-collection stages, but its core loop is puzzle-solving with tools rather than arcade platform-combat.
Donkey Kong Bananza is a 3D exploratory collectathon, not a single-screen arcade platformer, so it weakens the rail substantially.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: single-screen/compact stage arcade platforming, enemy-focused clears, level-based structure, and strong couch co-op overlap. One of the clearest comps for Chickums.
Shares 2D level-based egg collection and compact stages, but the gameplay identity is puzzle-platforming with tool use and planning, not arcade action-platforming or enemy-clearing.
This specific title is a 3D collectathon/exploration platformer with expansive world traversal, far removed from Chickums’ single-screen arcade structure and challenge model.

title: The Abbess Garden

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently describe a single-player, third-person cozy gardening game set in historical France. The core loop is restoring and tending a garden in a free-form natural space, growing and identifying plants through simulated ecosystem factors like soil, moisture, and plant proximity, while progressing gentle quests, uncovering hidden objects, helping NPCs, and pursuing romance. The game is explicitly peaceful and non-combat, with linear story advancement layered onto an at-your-own-pace life/gardening routine. That aligns best with farming_sim, with life_sim as a secondary influence due to social/romance and community-help elements.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center around plant care, restoration, and cozy progression, but it mixes true gardening/restoration comps with puzzle-forward or broader farming-life sims that drift away from the anchor’s specific free-form garden-tending identity.
anchor_summary: A cozy single-player third-person gardening/restoration sim with free-form planting, realistic plant/ecosystem behavior, gentle questing, hidden-object discovery, linear story progression, and romance/community elements in a peaceful non-combat structure.
gap_notes: Best fits are games centered on gardening/restoration rather than broad farm-economy loops.
Several candidates match botany or mystery themes but are structurally more puzzle/adventure than life/gardening sim.
The set lacks another close third-person cozy restoration/gardening sim with social progression; the closest supplied option is clearly Garden Life.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest match on free-form gardening, restoring an overgrown space, relaxed pacing, neighbor tasks, unlocking tools/seeds/areas, and cozy non-combat progression.
Strong cozy life-sim overlap via gardening, helping townsfolk, romance, and story progression, though it is a fuller farming/crafting sim with more systems than the anchor.

Good fit on calm plant-growing, historical setting, exploration, and figuring out ideal growth conditions, but it is primarily a first-person environmental puzzle game rather than an ongoing gardening/life sim.

Reasonable broad cozy-sim comp through crop growing, restoration, quests, and relationships, but its open-ended farm management, combat/mine loop, and multiplayer profile make it less like the anchor’s focused peaceful garden restoration.
Understandable for plant identification and mystery, but the core loop is a desk/shop-based occult deduction puzzle, not hands-on garden restoration or cozy gardening simulation.

title: World of Warcraft: Midnight

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored text focuses almost entirely on setting, zones, and story context for Azeroth/Quel'Thalas/Xal'atath, without concrete gameplay loop, combat, progression, perspective, or mode details. The only direct gameplay-identity signal is a single Metacritic genre label of 'MMORPG', which is not sufficient on its own to confidently map this specific release to an allowed archetype such as mmo_action_rpg, especially since the record is not marked as DLC and therefore cannot inherit World of Warcraft parent evidence under the provided rules.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's likely MMORPG identity well, led by another modern WoW expansion and major theme-park MMO neighbors. The main issue is that it lacks the actual retail World of Warcraft base game and instead uses WoW Classic, which is related but not the correct base-game pairing for Midnight's gameplay context.
anchor_summary: World of Warcraft: Midnight appears to be a World of Warcraft MMORPG expansion/release tied to the live theme-park MMO loop: persistent character progression, questing in new zones, instanced group PvE, and expansion-driven endgame within a shared online world.
gap_notes: Missing the actual retail World of Warcraft base game/direct parent context; WoW Classic is not an exact substitute.
Top of rail is solid for MMORPG audience fit, but fit drops after the first three due to combat/progression model differences across MMOs.
All candidates are at least understandable genre neighbors; none are completely off-genre.

title: Lil Gator Game: Gator of the Year

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence is insufficient for classification. The bundle contains no gameplay description, no taxonomy text corpus, no genre/theme/mode labels with usable semantic meaning, and no parent-game context to inherit from. Available data is limited to platform labels and opaque theme IDs, which do not establish gameplay identity strongly enough for Taxonomy V2.

overall_note: The rail is well aligned around cozy exploration-first 3D adventures. It correctly includes the original Lil Gator Game as the essential comparison, with A Short Hike and Smushi Come Home as very strong gameplay neighbors. The lower two are still reasonable but drift a bit farther from the traversal-collectathon core.

gap_notes: Lil Gator Game is the clear must-include base-game match.

title: Soulshard

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Soulshard as a 2D side-scrolling platformer built around 30 discrete levels, trap avoidance, and using the environment to progress, with single-player and local co-op play. The copy does not establish meaningful combat, RPG systems, exploration structure, or action-platformer fighting, which points away from action_platformer and toward a non-combat precision/platform-navigation identity. Minimalist pixel-art presentation and a dark limbo-like setting are also directly supported by the store text.

overall_note: The candidate set is directionally solid: most picks understand the anchor as a co-op-capable, level-based 2D platformer centered on traversal and hazards rather than combat. The best matches are co-op puzzle/precision platformers with stage-based progression. The main weakness is that some candidates lean more heavily into bespoke puzzle-gimmicks or combat than the anchor evidence supports.
anchor_summary: Soulshard appears to be a non-combat 2D precision/puzzle-platformer built around discrete trap-heavy levels, environmental interaction, hazard avoidance, and optional local co-op, with a casual-to-moderate platforming profile rather than combat or exploration-driven progression.
gap_notes: No candidate looks like an exact same-identity match for a minimalist non-combat trap-avoidance platformer with optional local co-op.
Several candidates are stronger on forced co-op puzzle design than Soulshard, which appears playable solo and less systemically elaborate.
BattleBlock Theater is somewhat over-indexed on weapons/combat and broader mode variety compared with the anchor's likely simpler hazard-navigation loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on 2D co-op platforming, coordinated traversal, hazard navigation, and level-based structure; more puzzle-specialized and more explicitly co-op-dependent than Soulshard.
Strong fit on side-scrolling traversal, environmental obstacles, local co-op plus solo play, and low-combat challenge structure; less minimalist and more cinematic than the anchor.
Good match for trap-heavy stage challenges, precise movement, solo/couch co-op support, and platform-focused difficulty; somewhat more twitchy and puzzle-stunt driven.

Fits teamwork platforming, obstacle-course progression, and environmental interaction well, but is more gimmick-led and more strongly built around two-character cooperation than Soulshard seems to be.
Shares 2D stage-based co-op platforming and trap gauntlets, but its weapon-tools, combat emphasis, and broader arcade chaos make it a looser gameplay match.

title: Pokémon FireRed Version and Pokémon LeafGreen Version

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a classic Pokémon RPG centered on capturing wild Pokémon, building and training a collection, and battling Gym Leaders across the Kanto region. The text explicitly mentions turn-based gameplay, collecting the original 151 Pokémon, earning badges, developing battle strategies, and progressing through an authored regional adventure. That combination strongly matches monster_collect_rpg, with jrpg_story_rpg as a secondary fit due to its linear campaign structure and heroic journey framing.

overall_note: The proposed candidate is a very strong fit. It matches the anchor on the mainline Pokémon loop almost point for point: classic overworld exploration, turn-based monster battles, badge-gated progression, Pokédex collection, and faithful remake structure.

title: DRACU-RIOT!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies DRACU-RIOT! as a single-player visual novel/adventure with no combat emphasis and a linear authored story. Metacritic explicitly labels it 'Visual Novel'; Steam and the corpus provide only story/setup text centered on protagonist, characters, and setting, with no signs of action, strategy, simulation, or puzzle-forward play. The gameplay identity is therefore best matched as visual_novel.

overall_note: This rail is well targeted around gameplay identity rather than surface theme. The strongest candidates are other route-based romance visual novels with similarly reading-first structure and heroine-focused progression; only Aokana sits a bit farther out due to its stronger sports framing.

Aokana is still valid, but its sports/team-development framing makes it slightly less interchangeable with DRACU-RIOT! than the Yuzusoft romance-supernatural peers.

title: Aquamarine: Explorer's Edition

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently portray Aquamarine as a single-player, turn-based sci-fi survival/exploration adventure focused on foraging, cultivation, salvage, loadout upgrades, navigational challenges, and environmental puzzle solving in an alien ocean. The strongest supported signals are cursor-driven/point-and-click interaction, authored linear mystery framing, non-combat play, and environmental puzzles. However, none of the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes cleanly fits this combination. It is not well-supported as hidden_object_puzzle because the evidence emphasizes survival, navigation, and environmental problem solving rather than hidden-object scenes. It is not well-supported as management_tycoon because there is no strong business/optimization/tycoon loop. It also does not cleanly map to exploration_survival_adventure because the available archetype implies a more standard real-time survival-adventure identity than the game's turn-based, comic-book, puzzle-forward structure. Because the evidence is solid about what the game is, but does not strongly support any allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's survival-exploration side reasonably well, especially around expedition pressure, resource management, and discovery. The main gap is that Aquamarine is notably turn-based, puzzle-forward, and largely non-combat, while several candidates skew more toward action, party management, or narrative sailing systems than environmental problem solving.
anchor_summary: Aquamarine: Explorer's Edition is a single-player, turn-based survival/exploration adventure with strong environmental puzzle solving, resource upkeep, scavenging/cultivation/crafting, and mystery-driven progression in an alien ocean. Its identity is closer to a slow, non-combat, expedition-survival puzzle game than to a tycoon or hidden-object game.
gap_notes: No candidate closely matches Aquamarine's specific blend of turn-based survival, environmental puzzle solving, and low/no-combat progression.
Several picks match exploration and survival pressure but miss the anchor's puzzle-forward, authored-mystery structure.
The set leans toward broader survival/exploration comps rather than games with similar interaction style and challenge model.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best fit here: turn-based expedition structure, survival pressure, resource management, procedural hazards, and roguelike run tension align well, though it is more party/event driven and less puzzle-centric than Aquamarine.

Shares journey-based survival, supply management, hazard mitigation, and authored-progress campaign framing. It is less about environmental puzzle solving and alien-world exploration, but the survival-travel loop is meaningfully adjacent.

Very strong overlap in alien-ocean survival, scavenging, crafting upgrades, studying wildlife, and trying to get home. It falls short on gameplay identity because Subnautica is real-time, open-world, and more action-driven than Aquamarine's turn-based, puzzle-forward structure.

Matches hostile-world exploration, resource upkeep, narrative discovery, and gradual lore uncovering, but its sailing/trading/RPG structure, frequent combat pressure, and legacy progression make it a looser fit for Aquamarine's turn-based survival-puzzle loop.

title: Resident Evil Requiem

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions repeatedly frame the game as 'survival horror' and add concrete loop details: combat, investigations, puzzles, and resource management. The corpus also explicitly says players can freely switch between first- and third-person views, supporting a modern single-player campaign survival-horror structure rather than a purely cinematic or purely psychological horror title. Story setup emphasizes investigating mysterious deaths in Raccoon City while escaping lethal threats, which supports detective_mystery plus survival_escape within an authored linear campaign.

overall_note: The set contains several credible modern survival-horror comps, led by Resident Evil 2 and Resident Evil Village, but it is weakened by one clear title mismatch/DLC pick and by some drift toward more action-heavy neighbors.
anchor_summary: Resident Evil Requiem appears to be a modern single-player survival horror campaign built around tense combat, investigation, puzzle gating, resource management, and an authored story, with both first- and third-person play and a mix of classic horror pressure plus some action-forward stretches.
gap_notes: The biggest issue is that the first candidate is not Resident Evil 7: Biohazard but the End of Zoe DLC, which has a meaningfully different, more melee/action-focused loop.
Top of rail quality is good, but the list is not consistently centered on investigation-puzzle-resource-management survival horror.
Some candidates fit best as adjacent or secondary comps rather than core identity matches for Requiem's classic survival-horror framing.

candidate_reviews.rationale: A very close match on core loop: tense exploration, limited resources, puzzle solving, authored campaigns, and survival-horror combat pressure in a modern Resident Evil format.

Strong modern RE survival-horror comp with inventory pressure, linear campaign progression, and combat/horror balance, though it leans more action-forward than the anchor’s classic investigative framing.
Good third-person survival-horror neighbor with combat, stealth, limited ammo, exploration, and narrative progression, but its semi-open structure and stealth/trap emphasis make it a looser fit.
Relevant especially for Requiem’s Leon/action-horror side, but RE4’s set-piece pacing and heavier combat emphasis sit somewhat farther from classic puzzle-gated survival-horror identity.

title: Laysara: Summit Kingdom

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Laysara: Summit Kingdom as a 'challenging city builder' and a 'pure city building experience' focused on economy, resource management, production chains, settlement expansion, transport/logistics optimization, hazard mitigation, and rebuilding across multiple mountain towns. The core loop is establishing and expanding settlements, satisfying population needs, building trade and transport networks, and solving environmental constraints like avalanches and weather. The text also directly states there is no combat or military aspect. This strongly supports city_builder as the primary archetype, with management_tycoon/colony_sim as adjacent secondary readings due to resource chains, needs management, and multi-settlement administration.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's city-building, logistics, and environment-pressure identity fairly well, but it leans heavily toward survival-flavored builders and misses a clearer pure-economy/logistics peer. The best fits are games that combine settlement planning, supply chains, terrain or hazard management, and multi-settlement or network thinking without shifting too far into combat or narrative survival drama.
anchor_summary: Laysara: Summit Kingdom is a combat-free, single-player city builder centered on settlement growth, production chains, transport-network optimization, population-needs management, and surviving harsh environmental constraints across multiple interconnected mountain towns.
gap_notes: The set overindexes on survival-city-builder pressure relative to Laysara's more economy-and-logistics-first identity.
A closer pure transport/production-chain city builder would strengthen the rail more than another scenario-driven survival game.
Several candidates fit on hazards or settlement management, but fewer match the anchor's specific multi-town trade-network emphasis.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on city-building fundamentals: multiple settlements, hostile weather, needs balancing, production chains, and persistent network-level progression. The main mismatch is its roguelite run structure versus Laysara's steadier sandbox/campaign buildout.

Excellent fit on economy design, citizen-need ladders, inter-settlement trade, and logistics optimization. It is less survival-focused and includes diplomacy/warfare and broader empire play, but the production-network identity is very relevant.

Strong overlap in terrain-aware building, infrastructure routing, environmental hazard preparation, and sandbox settlement optimization. It is more colony-survival and water/terraforming-driven than Laysara's multi-town mountain trade focus.

A solid combat-free settlement survival builder with resource planning, population management, and town sustainability. It is a simpler, more traditional single-settlement survival sim with less emphasis on transport engineering and interconnected settlements.

Matches harsh-climate city survival and infrastructure/resource management, but Frostpunk is much more scenario-driven, morality-law focused, and survival-narrative heavy than Laysara's pure economy/logistics city-building sandbox.

title: The New Zealand Story: Untold Adventure

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a colorful 2D platformer and remake of the 1988 platformer The NewZealand Story. The gameplay loop is described as jumping and running through handcrafted levels, uncovering hidden areas, using weapons, vehicles, and power-ups, and facing challenging enemies and bosses. This supports a level-based side-scrolling action-platformer identity with platform navigation as the core verb and combat as a supporting but meaningful layer. Although the computed near-miss/candidate system surfaced beat_em_up from side-scrolling + melee + bosses, the text strongly centers platforming, responsive movement, precision, hidden secrets, and arcade stage challenge rather than crowd-control brawling, making action_platformer the better fit.

overall_note: The rail has the right general neighborhood: classic and revival-era 2D action-platformers with level-based progression and old-school challenge. Best fits are arcade-rooted stage-clear platformers; the weakest entry is the multi-game Wonder Boy compilation because its included games span several gameplay identities rather than matching the anchor cleanly.
anchor_summary: A single-player 2D side-scrolling action-platformer built around stage progression, precision movement, enemies, bosses, hidden areas, and arcade-style challenge, with weapons/vehicles as supporting mechanics rather than brawler-style combat.
gap_notes: Strongest comparisons should emphasize arcade-style 2D action-platformers with discrete stages, hazards, enemy pressure, bosses, and precision jumping.
The set leans well into retro revivals, but one candidate is a broad franchise collection rather than a single gameplay identity.
No obvious direct/base-game pairing exists here, so the rail depends on close genre neighbors rather than essential must-include matches.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: arcade-origin 2D action-platforming, stage clears, projectile combat, hazards, and boss encounters align strongly with the anchor’s core loop.

candidate_reviews.candidate_title: Alex Kidd in Miracle World DX
candidate_reviews.requested_title: Alex Kidd in Miracle World DX
candidate_reviews.rationale: Another classic-platformer revival with level-based progression, boss fights, old-school difficulty, and modernized controls; slightly looser due to its distinctive rock-paper-scissors boss structure.
Excellent 2D action-platformer fundamentals with responsive movement, secrets, and level-based play, but it is more fluid and modern set-piece driven than the anchor’s retro arcade challenge profile.
Understandable due to classic platformer lineage, but as a compilation spanning straight platforming, action-RPG, and other variants, it lacks a single tight gameplay identity matching the anchor.

title: The Demon Queen's Dire Dilemma

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is strong and direct for a visual novel classification. Multiple store/editorial descriptions explicitly call it a 'visual novel' and describe '60k of text,' '2-4 hours of reading,' and 'a linear narrative with no choices or branches.' The gameplay identity is therefore narrative-reading focused rather than action, strategy, or exploration driven. Supporting facets include single-player mode, cute anime-style presentation, casual genre labeling, and absence of combat mechanics. The premise centers on character interaction between the demon queen and princess, supporting an authored linear interpersonal drama structure within a fantasy setting.

overall_note: The set is directionally correct because all candidates are yuri story-led visual novels centered on reading and character relationships. The main gap is structural precision: the anchor is explicitly short and linear with no choices, so kinetic or otherwise tightly linear VNs fit best, while more branching route-based titles are a bit looser.
anchor_summary: A short, single-player yuri visual novel with a linear, no-choices reading structure, anime presentation, comedic character banter, and no combat or exploration-driven gameplay.
gap_notes: The rail leans heavily on shared yuri/romance theme, which is helpful but should remain secondary to VN structure and interaction model.
Best fits are linear or near-linear reading-first visual novels; branching/multiple-ending candidates are somewhat less exact.
No candidate matches the anchor's exact comedic fantasy demon/princess premise, but that is less important than the shared no-combat, text-driven VN identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: A short yuri kinetic/linear visual novel with a reading-first structure, no action layer, and strong focus on dialogue and relationship payoff; closest match to the anchor's core loop.

Another prominent yuri VN built around reading and character interactions, with very close audience expectations, though its broader ensemble/romance-management framing is a bit less exact than the anchor's tight linear setup.
Clearly a yuri visual novel with the same text-heavy, anime-style format, but its branching story arcs and multiple endings make it less structurally similar to the anchor's no-choices linear design.
